sub_criteria.name: Common Characteristics of Good Applications
sub_criteria.definition: The application demonstrates characteristics commonly found in strong grant applications.

signals.signal_text: Clear research vision and career trajectory
Distinctive ideas with an independent direction
Well-justified training plan
Strong mentorship and institutional support

sub_criteria.pros: Clear research vision (academic dermatologist) and career trajectory, well-justified and structured training plan, strong mentorship and institutional support.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: Distinctive ideas with independent direction are not strongly evidenced; the research direction aligns with supervisor expertise rather than demonstrating novelty.
sub_criteria.rationale: Distinctive ideas with independent direction are not strongly evidenced; the research direction aligns with supervisor expertise rather than demonstrating novelty.

sub_criteria.name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award
sub_criteria.definition: The applicant clearly articulates why this specific award is the right next step in their career.
sub_criteria.group_name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award

signals.signal_text: Explain why this award is the right next step in your career (not just another project grant)
What difference would securing an award make to your career, and why now?
How will an award build on your existing experience and what new skills will it enable you to gain?
How will it develop your research partnerships within the NHS, public health or social care, charity organisations, industry or other organisations?

sub_criteria.pros: Clearly explains why the award is the right next step (e.g., building on prior experience, developing partnerships via BADBIR and guideline group).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: Less explicit on 'why now' and the specific new skills to be gained; could further emphasize career difference and immediate need.
sub_criteria.rationale: Less explicit on 'why now' and the specific new skills to be gained; could further emphasize career difference and immediate need.

sub_criteria.name: Relevant Research Experience and Training
sub_criteria.definition: Demonstrates prior research experience and/or training that prepares the applicant to successfully undertake the proposed award in applied health or social care research.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of previous projects, research methods, or techniques relevant to the proposed study
Evidence of formal training, courses, or certifications
Alignment between prior experience and skills required for the proposed project

sub_criteria.pros: Extensive prior research experience with relevant techniques (e.g., single-cell sequencing, survival analysis) and formal training including a PGDip and specialized courses; strong alignment with the proposed project's methods.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: None significant; all signals are robustly supported.
sub_criteria.rationale: None significant; all signals are robustly supported.

sub_criteria.name: Impactful Research Outputs and Outcomes
sub_criteria.definition: Evidence of meaningful research outputs or outcomes appropriate to the applicant’s career stage, demonstrating contribution to the field.

signals.signal_text: Number and quality of publications, reports, or presentations
Citation metrics or other indicators of impact (where mentioned)
Awards, grants, or recognitions linked to prior research
Description of practical or societal impact of previous work

sub_criteria.pros: Strong publication record (14 papers, 8 first author, h-index 9), multiple awards/prizes, and a £25k grant indicate impactful outputs and recognition.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: Description of practical or societal impact of previous work is limited; only h-index provided as impact metric, and no specific examples of real-world application.
sub_criteria.rationale: Description of practical or societal impact of previous work is limited; only h-index provided as impact metric, and no specific examples of real-world application.

sub_criteria.name: Trajectory Towards Research Leadership
sub_criteria.definition: Demonstrates potential and progression towards becoming a future leader in health or social care research.

signals.signal_text: Statements of career ambitions and long-term goals
Evidence of increasing responsibility in previous roles (e.g., leading projects, mentoring)
Participation in leadership or professional development programs
Recognition by peers or institutions suggesting leadership potential

sub_criteria.pros: Clear career ambitions (academic dermatologist, clinician scientist), evidence of increasing responsibility (leading projects, supervising, national committee), and participation in leadership programs (Emerging Clinical Leaders, Future Leaders Academy).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: Recognition by peers/institutions is strong but could be more explicit with external leadership awards or senior roles.
sub_criteria.rationale: Recognition by peers/institutions is strong but could be more explicit with external leadership awards or senior roles.

sub_criteria.name: Commitment to Practitioner-Academic Career (if applicable)
sub_criteria.definition: For applicants including a clinical or practice element, shows a clear commitment to combining research with professional practice.

signals.signal_text: Explicit statement of practitioner-academic career intentions
Involvement in clinical or practice-based research
Evidence of balancing practice responsibilities with research activities
Participation in relevant professional networks or qualifications

sub_criteria.pros: Explicit commitment to a practitioner-academic career (AFP, ACF, goal of academic dermatologist) and strong involvement in clinical and practice-based research.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: Limited evidence of balancing practice responsibilities with research (e.g., part-time arrangements) and no detailed mention of professional networks or qualifications beyond GMC registration.
sub_criteria.rationale: Limited evidence of balancing practice responsibilities with research (e.g., part-time arrangements) and no detailed mention of professional networks or qualifications beyond GMC registration.

sub_criteria.name: Quality of the Plain English Summary
sub_criteria.definition: Clear, simple description of the research that a non-specialist audience can understand; explains problem, importance, plan, beneficiaries, and real-world use; avoids jargon; aligns with the detailed proposal.

signals.signal_text: Readability scores (e.g., Flesch-Kincaid)
Low density of unexplained jargon or technical terms
Presence of problem statement, objectives, methods, beneficiaries, and real-world impact
Alignment with detailed proposal content
Sentence structure and coherence for non-specialist understanding
Emphasis on societal or patient relevance

sub_criteria.pros: The Plain English Summary covers all required components (problem, objectives, methods, beneficiaries, impact), is well-structured and coherent, uses simple language with minimal unexplained jargon, and strongly emphasizes patient and societal relevance. Alignment with the detailed proposal is clear.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: Flesch-Kincaid grade (10.58) is slightly high for a lay audience; some terms like 'systematic review' could be explained more explicitly.
sub_criteria.rationale: Flesch-Kincaid grade (10.58) is slightly high for a lay audience; some terms like 'systematic review' could be explained more explicitly.

sub_criteria.name: Evidence Review Quality & Relevance
sub_criteria.definition: How comprehensively and critically the application reviews existing literature and evidence.

signals.signal_text: Number and relevance of references cited
Presence of critical analysis vs. mere summary
Identification of knowledge gaps
Coherence and logical flow

sub_criteria.pros: Clear identification of knowledge gaps (no comprehensive summary, no predictive tool), critical analysis of existing studies (e.g., limitations of real-world drug persistence data), and logical flow from problem to proposed review.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: Number of cited references in the provided text is limited; a more extensive reference list would strengthen evidence.
sub_criteria.rationale: Number of cited references in the provided text is limited; a more extensive reference list would strengthen evidence.

sub_criteria.name: Research Scope & Award Appropriateness
sub_criteria.definition: Whether the project scope matches the scale and expectations of the proposed award (doctoral, postdoctoral, etc.).

signals.signal_text: Alignment of aims with award level
Feasibility indicators (time, complexity, tasks)
Proportion of work relative to award expectations

sub_criteria.pros: Aims are well-aligned with a fellowship award; phased timeline (0-36 months) and risk mitigation strategies indicate feasibility; scope is appropriate for the award level.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: Specific sample size justifications and detailed feasibility calculations are not fully visible in the provided excerpts.
sub_criteria.rationale: Specific sample size justifications and detailed feasibility calculations are not fully visible in the provided excerpts.

sub_criteria.name: Research Design & Methodological Rigor
sub_criteria.definition: Clarity, appropriateness, and robustness of study design to answer the research questions.

signals.signal_text: Explicit description of methods
Logical alignment between aims and methods
Discussion of controls, sample size, or methodology justification
Structured methodological sections

sub_criteria.pros: Methods are explicitly described (systematic review, penalized regression, focus groups), logically aligned with aims, and structured into clear phases. The design is rigorous and appropriate.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: Sample size justification and control definitions are not elaborated in the provided text, leaving some methodological detail gaps.
sub_criteria.rationale: Sample size justification and control definitions are not elaborated in the provided text, leaving some methodological detail gaps.

sub_criteria.name: Potential Impact on Patients, Public, and Health Services
sub_criteria.definition: Likely benefits or influence of the research on patients, carers, or health and care systems.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of direct or indirect patient/public benefits
Alignment with health priorities or societal needs
Clarity and specificity of expected outcomes

sub_criteria.pros: Direct patient benefits (reduced anxiety, personalized management) and health service impacts (efficient resource use) are clearly stated and aligned with UK health priorities (NICE guidelines).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: Expected outcomes could be more specific and measurable (e.g., quantified reductions in appointments).
sub_criteria.rationale: Expected outcomes could be more specific and measurable (e.g., quantified reductions in appointments).

sub_criteria.name: Engagement with People & Communities
sub_criteria.definition: Evidence of meaningful involvement of patients, carers, or communities.

signals.signal_text: Use of co-design, participatory methods, or consultations
References to ethical considerations and feedback mechanisms
Description of involvement at multiple project stages

sub_criteria.pros: Strong engagement through co-design focus groups, beta testing, and an ongoing PPI advisory group. Involvement spans design, analysis, and dissemination. Feedback mechanisms (6-monthly meetings) are in place.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: Ethical considerations regarding PPI are not explicitly tied to the engagement plan, though overall approach is robust.
sub_criteria.rationale: Ethical considerations regarding PPI are not explicitly tied to the engagement plan, though overall approach is robust.

sub_criteria.name: Justification of Resources & Award Costs
sub_criteria.definition: Appropriateness and clarity of requested resources for the planned work.

signals.signal_text: Explicit links between tasks and resources requested
Budget proportionality to project scope
Clear rationale for funding level

sub_criteria.pros: Budget items (courses, data management, statistician) are explicitly linked to project tasks, and justifications are provided. The budget appears proportional to the project scope.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No explicit justification for the total funding level; proportionality is inferred but not stated.
sub_criteria.rationale: No explicit justification for the total funding level; proportionality is inferred but not stated.

sub_criteria.name: Quality of Previous Research (if applicable)
sub_criteria.definition: Prior relevant research experience demonstrating capability to deliver the proposed project.

signals.signal_text: Number and relevance of publications or outputs
Evidence of prior successful projects
Skills alignment with proposed work

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant has undertaken relevant training (e.g., genetic epidemiology, qualitative interviewing), indicating some skill alignment with the proposed work.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No evidence of prior publications, successful projects, or research outputs in the provided application text. The signal for skills alignment is weak as training alone does not demonstrate prior research quality.
sub_criteria.rationale: No evidence of prior publications, successful projects, or research outputs in the provided application text. The signal for skills alignment is weak as training alone does not demonstrate prior research quality.

sub_criteria.name: Inclusive Research Design
sub_criteria.definition: Consideration of inclusivity throughout the research lifecycle, including participant diversity and accessibility.

signals.signal_text: Explicit strategies for diverse recruitment
Adaptive methods or accessibility considerations
Equity and participation indicators

sub_criteria.pros: Explicit strategies to recruit from underserved groups (lower socioeconomic, ethnic minorities, disabilities) and use of an inclusion toolkit. Digital exclusion is addressed.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: Adaptive methods and specific equity indicators could be described in more detail.
sub_criteria.rationale: Adaptive methods and specific equity indicators could be described in more detail.

sub_criteria.name: Quality and Relevance of Proposed Training
sub_criteria.definition: How well-structured, clear, and relevant the training plan is in relation to the project’s goals and the applicant’s research needs.

signals.signal_text: Completeness and clarity of training description
Alignment of training topics with project methods or techniques
Structured timeline or sequencing of training activities
Explicit links between training content and skill acquisition

sub_criteria.pros: Training plan is highly detailed, clearly described, and directly aligned with project methods. Timeline is structured across years, and each course explicitly states the skills to be acquired.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: None; the training description is comprehensive and well-integrated.
sub_criteria.rationale: None; the training description is comprehensive and well-integrated.

sub_criteria.name: Suitability to Personal Development and Project Needs
sub_criteria.definition: How well the proposed training addresses the applicant’s current skills gaps and supports successful project delivery.

signals.signal_text: Identification of personal skill gaps
Clear mapping between training activities and project tasks
Evidence that proposed training is targeted rather than generic
Mention of mentorship, workshops, or formal courses relevant to development needs

sub_criteria.pros: Applicant explicitly identifies skill gaps (e.g., need for formal statistics training) and maps each course to specific project tasks. Training is targeted (e.g., predictive modeling course) and includes mentorship and formal courses.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: None; the mapping is clear and evidence is strong.
sub_criteria.rationale: None; the mapping is clear and evidence is strong.

sub_criteria.name: Support for Career Aspirations
sub_criteria.definition: The extent to which the training plan, and any professional practice components, contribute to the applicant’s long-term career goals.

signals.signal_text: Explicit statement of career goals
Connections between training activities and future career trajectory
Indications of skill transferability beyond the current project
Reference to professional practice or experiential learning where applicable

sub_criteria.pros: Career goal of becoming an academic dermatologist is explicitly stated. Leadership training (Emerging Clinical Leaders, Future Leaders Academy) is directly tied to postdoctoral career trajectory.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: Transferability of skills beyond the project is implied but not extensively detailed. Integration with professional practice (clinical work) is not explicitly addressed.
sub_criteria.rationale: Transferability of skills beyond the project is implied but not extensively detailed. Integration with professional practice (clinical work) is not explicitly addressed.

sub_criteria.name: Track Record of the Contracting Organisation (and Partners)
sub_criteria.definition: Demonstrated expertise, experience, and research achievements of the organisation(s) in the proposed research area.

signals.signal_text: Publication or project history in relevant research fields
Previous funding success or awards in similar domains
Mention of prior collaborations or partnerships
Highlighted achievements or notable outputs

sub_criteria.pros: The St John's Institute of Dermatology is described as a world-renowned centre with an outstanding track record of supporting PhD fellows, a >95% fellowship success rate, high research income, and top REF ratings. It also has well-established links with patient associations and PPI networks, demonstrating strong track record and collaborations.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: Only general achievements are cited; specific publication lists or prior project details are not provided, and collaborations are mentioned only briefly.
sub_criteria.rationale: Only general achievements are cited; specific publication lists or prior project details are not provided, and collaborations are mentioned only briefly.

sub_criteria.name: Suitability of Supervisory Support and Mentorship Arrangements
sub_criteria.definition: Quality, clarity, and appropriateness of supervision and mentoring for supporting the applicant’s research and career development.

signals.signal_text: Explicit description of supervisor roles and responsibilities
Evidence of mentorship plans, meetings, or training support
Alignment between supervisor expertise and project needs
Presence of structured supervision framework (frequency, format, accountability)

sub_criteria.pros: Each supervisor has explicit roles and responsibilities, with expertise directly aligned to the project (e.g., Dr Mahil in psoriasis, Dr Norton in statistics). A structured supervision framework includes weekly/fortnightly meetings, a progression committee, and a Patient and Lay Advisory Group, providing comprehensive mentorship and accountability.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; all signals strongly supported.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; all signals strongly supported.

sub_criteria.name: Appropriateness of the Contracting Organisation to Support the Award and Career Aspirations
sub_criteria.definition: How well the organisation (and any partners) can provide the infrastructure, resources, and environment necessary to deliver the project and support the applicant’s career growth.

signals.signal_text: Description of facilities, infrastructure, or resources provided
Links between organisational support and project success
Alignment of organisational focus with applicant’s career trajectory
Evidence of tailored support for award type (doctoral, postdoctoral)

sub_criteria.pros: The contracting organisation provides state-of-the-art facilities, a world-renowned research environment, and a comprehensive Doctoral Development Programme. The institution is a natural fit for the applicant's career as an academic dermatologist and has a proven track record of supporting clinical academic PhD fellows.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: Links between specific resources and project success could be more explicit, but overall support is strong.
sub_criteria.rationale: Links between specific resources and project success could be more explicit, but overall support is strong.

sub_criteria.name: Commitment to Inclusive and Supportive Research Culture
sub_criteria.definition: Evidence that the organisation fosters inclusivity, research integrity, and a supportive environment for all researchers.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of policies or practices promoting diversity, equity, and inclusion
Evidence of inclusive recruitment, mentoring, or support initiatives
References to adherence to research integrity principles
Statements on organisational culture, training, or initiatives supporting underrepresented groups

sub_criteria.pros: King's College London holds an Athena SWAN Silver award, indicating commitment to gender equality and inclusive research culture. The application also mentions research integrity principles.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: Specific initiatives for underrepresented groups, inclusive recruitment practices, or detailed diversity policies are not described. The evidence for commitment to inclusive culture is limited to the Athena SWAN award and a general statement on research integrity.
sub_criteria.rationale: Specific initiatives for underrepresented groups, inclusive recruitment practices, or detailed diversity policies are not described. The evidence for commitment to inclusive culture is limited to the Athena SWAN award and a general statement on research integrity.

sub_criteria.name: Integration of Lived Experience in Research Design
sub_criteria.definition: How input from patients, carers, or people with relevant lived experience has shaped the research questions, design, and methods.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of consultation or co-design activities
Specific examples of changes made to study design based on lived experience
References to patient or community advisory groups

sub_criteria.pros: Extensive and specific evidence of lived experience integration: initial PPI advisory meeting refined proposal; patients helped shape research questions and methods; ongoing Patient and Lay Advisory Group co-designs focus groups, topic guides, and analysis.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; all signals are strongly and directly evidenced.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; all signals are strongly and directly evidenced.

sub_criteria.name: Diversity and Inclusivity of the Research Team
sub_criteria.definition: Extent to which the research team reflects diversity in terms of skills, experience, and demographic representation.

signals.signal_text: Explicit mentions of team composition across gender, ethnicity, age, or other relevant characteristics
Statements about inclusion in hiring or team-building
Evidence of equitable task allocation and collaboration

sub_criteria.pros: None.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No mention of diversity (gender, ethnicity, age, etc.) in the research team composition, nor any statement about inclusive hiring or equitable task allocation.
sub_criteria.rationale: No mention of diversity (gender, ethnicity, age, etc.) in the research team composition, nor any statement about inclusive hiring or equitable task allocation.

sub_criteria.name: Commitment to Maximising Public and Patient Benefit
sub_criteria.definition: Clarity and strength of the plan to ensure research outcomes meaningfully benefit patients, carers, and the public.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of dissemination, knowledge translation, or implementation plans
Clear links between study objectives and anticipated benefits
Emphasis on actionable outcomes or improvements in health/care

sub_criteria.pros: Clear dissemination plan (conferences, journals, patient-friendly summaries via charities and social media); study objectives directly linked to reducing uncertainty, empowering lifestyle changes, and personalised care; actionable outcome (risk stratification tool).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; commitment to public and patient benefit is well articulated.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; commitment to public and patient benefit is well articulated.

sub_criteria.name: Representation of Seldom Heard or Underserved Groups
sub_criteria.definition: Degree to which participants and PPI contributors represent groups historically underrepresented in research, including those facing health inequalities.

signals.signal_text: Mention of specific demographic groups (age, race, ethnicity, disability, gender, sexual orientation, religion/belief, location, socioeconomic status)
Evidence of targeted recruitment strategies
Discussion of barriers to participation and mitigation strategies

sub_criteria.pros: Explicitly targets seldom heard groups: lower socioeconomic status, ethnic minorities, disabilities; uses a skin research inclusion toolkit; PPI group includes diversity in age, ethnicity, geography; mitigates digital exclusion with practice calls and phone-in options.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; representation and targeted strategies are well documented.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; representation and targeted strategies are well documented.

sub_criteria.name: Inclusivity Across the Research Lifecycle
sub_criteria.definition: Demonstration that inclusion is considered at all stages of the project: planning, recruitment, conduct, analysis, and dissemination.

signals.signal_text: References to inclusion in study design, participant selection, and analysis
Consideration of accessibility and equity in all project stages
Documented plans for ongoing evaluation and adaptation

sub_criteria.pros: Inclusion is planned across the entire research lifecycle: variable selection, recruitment, co-facilitation of focus groups, data interpretation, and dissemination; accessibility addressed (virtual meetings, phone-in, digital inclusion).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No explicit plan for ongoing evaluation or adjustment of inclusion strategies over the project period; while feedback loops exist, systematic monitoring is not documented.
sub_criteria.rationale: No explicit plan for ongoing evaluation or adjustment of inclusion strategies over the project period; while feedback loops exist, systematic monitoring is not documented.

sub_criteria.name: Costed and Resourced Plan for Research Inclusion
sub_criteria.definition: Evidence that research inclusion has been properly budgeted and resourced within the proposal.

signals.signal_text: Explicit budget line(s) for inclusion activities (PPI, outreach, recruitment support)
Mention of staff time, materials, or training dedicated to inclusion
Alignment between inclusion plan and allocated resources

sub_criteria.pros: PPI costs explicitly budgeted (advisory group meetings, co-facilitation, training) and aligned with NIHR guidance; staff time and training for inclusivity are included; resources directly match planned inclusion activities.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; costed plan is clear and proportional.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; costed plan is clear and proportional.

sub_criteria.name: Use of Formatting, Headings, and Subheadings
sub_criteria.definition: Effective use of text formatting to make the application clear, navigable, and easy to read.

signals.signal_text: Presence and consistency of headings and subheadings
Use of bold or emphasis for key terms
Structured hierarchy of sections (logical nesting of headings)
Patterns detected via text parsing for formatting markers

sub_criteria.pros: The application has clear section headings and a logical hierarchy, with 10 detected sections and 13 numbered heading-like lines. 38 emphasis markers and 65 bullet/list markers indicate good formatting practices.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: The parser notes that bold/emphasis may be lost, so actual emphasis usage may be higher than detected; some subheadings might not be fully consistent across sections (no direct evidence of consistency).
sub_criteria.rationale: The parser notes that bold/emphasis may be lost, so actual emphasis usage may be higher than detected; some subheadings might not be fully consistent across sections (no direct evidence of consistency).

sub_criteria.name: Limited Duplication and Repetition
sub_criteria.definition: Avoidance of unnecessary repetition within or across sections.

signals.signal_text: Semantic similarity measures between sentences/paragraphs
Detection of repeated phrases or ideas
Novelty score for each section (unique content vs. overlap)

sub_criteria.pros: Extremely low duplication rates: duplicate sentence rate only 0.3% and no repeated lines; lexical overlaps between sections are moderate (0.18-0.24), indicating mostly unique content.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: Some lexical overlap exists, especially between Training & Development and Budget (0.243) and Research Plan and Training (0.241), which could be improved but is not excessive.
sub_criteria.rationale: Some lexical overlap exists, especially between Training & Development and Budget (0.243) and Research Plan and Training (0.241), which could be improved but is not excessive.

sub_criteria.name: Logical Flow and Coherence Across Sections
sub_criteria.definition: The application progresses logically, with a clear thread linking the research question, objectives, and work packages.

signals.signal_text: Semantic coherence between sections (using embeddings or similarity metrics)
Explicit linking of objectives to methods and work packages
Presence of transitional phrases or linking statements
Detectable narrative thread anchored on research aims

sub_criteria.pros: High number of transitional phrases (78) and objective-method-budget link terms (151) indicate strong coherence and explicit linking; section order follows a logical progression.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: Semantic coherence measures are not directly provided; the narrative thread could be more explicitly tied to research aims across all sections.
sub_criteria.rationale: Semantic coherence measures are not directly provided; the narrative thread could be more explicitly tied to research aims across all sections.
